draft.introduction: Fragment 50 contains Heraclitus’s most direct statement on the unity underlying all things. The Logos speaks, not Heraclitus himself, and it declares that all things are one—a claim that echoed through centuries of Greek metaphysics.

translation.text: Listening not to me but to the Logos, it is wise to agree that all things are one.
translation.original_text: οὐκ ἐμοῦ ἀλλὰ τοῦ λόγου ἀκούσαντας ὁμολογεῖν σοφόν ἐστιν ἓν πάντα εἶναι

translator_notes.note: The distinction between listening to Heraclitus and listening to the Logos itself anticipates the Stoic idea that wisdom means aligning oneself with the rational order of the cosmos rather than with any individual teacher.
